items.correct: Attributing human emotions or qualities to nature or inanimate objects to reflect a character's feelings or scene's mood (e.g. angry storms during conflict).
items.misconception: Pathetic fallacy is when an argument in a story is weak or sad.
items.random_wrong: Pathetic fallacy is when characters cry over silly things.
items.why: The term uses 'pathetic' in its old sense — pertaining to emotion (pathos) — not 'feeble'. Students misread it as a value judgement when it is a technical device coined by Ruskin.

items.correct: A fatal error or flaw in the tragic hero that leads to their downfall — sometimes a character defect, sometimes a mistake in judgement.
items.misconception: Hamartia is the tragic hero's evil personality.
items.random_wrong: Hamartia is the moment the tragic hero dies.
items.why: Tragic heroes are not villains. Hamartia is often a noble quality taken too far (Othello's love, Macbeth's ambition) or a misjudgement — not simple wickedness.

items.correct: A line of five iambs (unstressed-stressed syllable pairs), creating a ten-syllable line with the rhythm da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM.
items.misconception: Iambic pentameter is any line with ten syllables.
items.random_wrong: Iambic pentameter is when every line rhymes.
items.why: Syllable count alone doesn't make iambic pentameter — the stress pattern is essential. And iambic pentameter is usually unrhymed (blank verse) in Shakespeare's plays.

items.q: Spaceships and aliens suggest the genre.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Science fiction. “Romance” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A genre focused on a puzzle to solve is.
items.why: Correct answer: Mystery. “Horror” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many fiction genres are named here?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
